pattern: Replacing a value with a divisor of it never needs more than one step, because a divisor of a divisor is already a divisor of the original -- so every element can independently drop straight to the smallest value present in the array that divides it.
hints: An operation only ever replaces a value with some other value that is currently present in the array and evenly divides it -- and since a divisor of a divisor is still a divisor, chaining several operations together never reaches anything a single direct operation couldn't reach.
So the minimum achievable value for a given number x is simply the smallest value in the original array (including x itself) that evenly divides x.

Rather than checking every pair, sieve it: for every distinct value v present, walk its multiples up to the largest number in the array and record v as a candidate divisor for each multiple that hasn't already found a smaller one; then sum each element's recorded best divisor.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one replacement lowers the sum
statement example, two replacements chain through a shared divisor
statement example, no pair is divisible, sum unchanged
single element, nothing to replace it with
smallest value is not a divisor of the others
a long divisor chain: each value divides the next
maximum value alongside the smallest possible value
value 1 present, every element collapses to it

stress.description: random values across the full length and value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

complexity.time: O(V log V) where V is the maximum value
